summary: Section order is topologically sound (communication protocol -> data packets -> best-effort delivery -> TCP lifecycle) and the first two sections teach well with multi-frame figures. But the chain breaks at its last two links: best-effort delivery — the concept the whole lifecycle is motivated by — has no figure at all (just a one-sentence definition and a back-link), and the target section never illustrates the lifecycle itself. Its single frame is a concept-map roll-up (three prerequisite boxes pointing at a box that merely lists 'handshake opens / data flows, confirmed / goodbye closes'). A curious adult finishes the page knowing why TCP needs ceremony but never sees the handshake, the acknowledged data flow, or the two-way goodbye actually happen, so the target concept is asserted rather than taught.
gaps: Broken arc: the target section's only figure is a prerequisite-convergence diagram, not a depiction of the TCP connection lifecycle. The opening greeting where both sides agree, the data-plus-confirmation middle, and the two-sided goodbye — the very stages named in the definition — are never drawn. The closest substitute is the generic 4-rule figure in the communication-protocol section, which is explicitly a generic protocol, not TCP, and lacks the lifecycle's defining moves (mutual agreement to open, per-packet confirmation, two-way close, recovery from loss).

Unmet prerequisite: best-effort delivery is never illustrated. Its section contains only a definition sentence and a 'Builds on' link — no figure, no honest stub notice — even though graph/best-effort-delivery.json marks it non-atomic and sketches a clear figure plan (mail with no tracking: lost, duplicated, late, shuffled). The data-packets figure covers only delay and reordering; loss and duplication, the failures that justify the handshake and confirmations, are never shown anywhere.

Leap: the target frame introduces the word 'handshake' with no prior definition or visual referent; earlier sections only ever say 'hello'. Similarly, the closing claim that 'the unreliable net makes each [stage] necessary' is asserted but never demonstrated — the reader never sees TCP detect a lost packet and resend, so the causal link between best-effort delivery and the lifecycle's ceremony remains untaught.

Minor: the communication-protocol figure's rule script (hello / answer / send data / confirm) silently doubles as a proto-TCP lifecycle, but the target never maps its three stages back onto that script or extends it with the goodbye, leaving the reader to make the connection alone.
